factual-accuracy.requirement: States individualism scores: South Korea collectivist (IDV 18±5), Finland individualist (IDV 63±5), Mexico collectivist (IDV 30±5)
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response gives South Korea an individualism score of 18 and labels it collectivist, but it does not state Finland’s individualism score as 63±5 nor Mexico’s as 30±5. It also does not explicitly label Finland individualist and Mexico collectivist alongside those required IDV values.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States power distance scores: South Korea mid-high (PDI 60±10), Finland low (PDI 33±5), Mexico very high (PDI 81±10)
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly states South Korea’s power distance index as 60 and Finland’s as 33, but it does not state Mexico’s power distance score as ~81 (±10). Because the Mexico PDI value is missing, the full set of required power distance scores is not provided.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States uncertainty avoidance: South Korea very high (UAI 85±10), Finland moderate (UAI 59±10), Mexico high (UAI 82±10)
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response does not state uncertainty avoidance (UAI) values for South Korea, Finland, and Mexico, nor does it indicate that Korea is very high (~85), Finland moderate (~59), and Mexico high (~82). It mentions Finland’s power distance score (33) and Korea’s power distance (60), but provides no UAI figures or ranges.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Describes Classting as teacher-moderated or teacher-led class SNS with private counseling rooms/secret channels and coarse moderation controls
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response characterizes Classting as a teacher-gated, teacher-moderated class social network by referencing “Classting model; teacher as gatekeeper” and “Classting’s Private Counseling feature,” and it describes granular moderation/permission controls. It also mentions private counseling channels/1:1 messaging, aligning with private rooms/secret-channel style communication.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States Wilma has 95-98% coverage in Finnish basic education schools and handles attendance/grades/messaging
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response discusses Wilma in Finland but does not state that Wilma has 95–98% coverage in Finnish basic education schools. It also does not explicitly describe Wilma as handling attendance, grades, and messaging together with that coverage claim, so the required coverage-and-function statement is missing.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Mentions Google Classroom Mexico/Latin America deployments reached state scale (hundreds of thousands to 1M+ students) with private comments and teacher-controlled posting emphasized
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response discusses Mexico/Latin America broadly but does not mention Google Classroom deployments reaching state scale (hundreds of thousands to 1M+ students). It also does not emphasize Google Classroom’s private comments feature or teacher-controlled posting settings in that Latin America context. Therefore the required specific deployment-scale and feature emphasis is missing.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States anonymous peer assessment increases cognitive/higher-order feedback or improves final grades significantly versus identified review
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly cites studies (e.g., Panadero & Alqassab, 2019; Lu & Bol, 2007; a 2022 anonymous peer feedback study) stating anonymous peer assessment yields more critical/cognitive feedback and better performance/writing outcomes than identifiable review.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States anonymous question queues or LAQQ reduce apprehension and increase question submission versus public-only channels
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response discusses anonymity in peer feedback generally (e.g., “anonymity relieves social pressure,” “reduces interpersonal barriers,” and recommends anonymous peer review defaults for Korea/Mexico). However, it does not specifically mention anonymous question queues or LAQQ, nor does it explicitly claim they reduce apprehension and increase question submission versus public-only channels.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Explains that high power distance reduces willingness to publicly challenge peers or request help in digital learning environments
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response links high power distance to hierarchical teacher-centered norms and face-saving, noting students are “never contradicted” and “expected to speak up only when invited,” and it recommends private/anonymous channels to avoid public exposure. It also describes reluctance to post publicly due to fear of mistakes and preference for seeking help privately/through peers rather than openly challenging others.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Recommends masked/anonymous early drafts, teacher-approved public showcases, anonymous live Q&A, or teacher-moderated boards for Korean group workspaces
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response recommends multiple Korean-specific group-workspace patterns including anonymous peer evaluation, anonymous reporting for group issues, and teacher approval gates/teacher review of feedback before release, which align with masked early drafts and teacher-moderated boards.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Recommends named edits/contributions, visible cursors, open collaboration, or student self-organization for Finnish group workspaces
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response recommends Finnish group workspace features such as student self-selection for group formation, student-determined/rotating roles, open channels with student moderation, student-led conflict resolution, and identified peer evaluation. These constitute open collaboration and student self-organization patterns for Finnish group workspaces.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Recommends private-first design, mobile/offline editing, teacher-controlled visibility, or teacher-only posting initially for Mexican group workspaces
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response recommends Mexico-specific designs including offline-capable peer review forms (“Offline-capable peer review forms”), high teacher moderation/approval (“teacher approval required”), and private error visibility (“Private (teacher-student)”), which align with private-first and teacher-controlled visibility/teacher-only posting initially.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Recommends double-blind or anonymous peer review with rubric-guided stems or structured feedback prompts for Korea
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response recommends anonymous peer review for South Korea and specifies structured, template-based feedback with rubric-guided elements (e.g., “template-based feedback,” “anonymous peer review enabled by default,” and “anonymous within-group evaluation using rubric”). It also includes teacher review/approval before release, aligning with double-blind/anonymous, structured prompt-based peer review for Korea.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Recommends named peer review with feedback literacy scaffolding, exemplars, or process-focused critique for Finland
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response recommends identified (named) peer review as the default for Finland and adds scaffolding such as mandatory self-assessment, structured peer review circles with rotating roles, and an emphasis on tangible, honest, process/formative critique (e.g., Qridi-style formative focus). These elements function as feedback-literacy scaffolding and process-focused critique for the Finnish context.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Recommends two-tier identity (anonymous to peers, identified to teacher) or private-first peer review with structured stems for Mexico
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly recommends for Mexico that peer review be anonymous by default with teacher approval/moderation, and it specifies structured, rubric-based feedback stems (e.g., “positive aspect” before “area for improvement”). This matches a two-tier/private-first peer review approach with structured stems tailored to Mexico.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Claims Finland has higher power distance than Korea or Mexico, or claims Mexico has lower power distance than Finland
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states Finland has low power distance (e.g., “score 33 on Hofstede's index”) and describes Mexico as high power distance, and it never claims Finland’s power distance is higher than Korea’s or Mexico’s, nor that Mexico’s is lower than Finland’s.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Predicts anonymity or power-distance interventions will show stronger effects in high-PDI markets (Korea/Mexico) versus low-PDI (Finland)
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response explicitly recommends anonymity defaults and higher teacher moderation for South Korea and Mexico while recommending identified feedback and low moderation for Finland, tying these defaults to high vs. low power distance and face-saving norms. This constitutes a prediction that anonymity/power-distance interventions will have stronger effects in high-PDI markets than in low-PDI Finland.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Describes temporal rollout strategies (private-first then gradual opening) with triggers or norms that enable stage transitions
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response does not describe a temporal rollout plan (e.g., private-first then gradual opening) for collaboration/feedback features. It mentions tiered moderation presets and “tiered systems” conceptually, but provides no staged deployment sequence with explicit triggers or norms for moving between stages.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Identifies negative consequences of recommended patterns (e.g., fairness concerns) and proposes compensating design elements or safeguards
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response explicitly discusses negative consequences such as the “anonymity paradox” (toxicity, accountability deficits, fairness/psychological safety concerns) and risks of high teacher moderation reducing engagement, then proposes safeguards like teacher review queues, AI sentiment flagging, feedback-literacy training, tiered moderation presets, and de-identified/aggregated error displays.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Specifies measurable outcomes beyond raw counts (higher-order comments, revision adoption rates) with expected effect ranges from research
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response includes some measurable outcomes (e.g., writing posttest scores for anonymous vs. identified peer review, telesecundaria effect sizes in SD and percentage points, and a 22% task-completion improvement). However, it does not specify higher-order collaboration outcomes like revision adoption rates or higher-order comment rates, nor does it provide expected effect ranges from research for engagement/peer-interaction/completion metrics across the cultural contexts.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Connects technical constraints (offline capability, bandwidth limits) to required collaboration design patterns in specific markets
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response explicitly links bandwidth/connectivity and offline constraints to collaboration design patterns by market, especially for Mexico (offline-capable peer review forms, print workflows, WhatsApp/SMS notifications) and notes Finland’s rural connectivity gap and Korea’s emerging access inequalities, tying these to multi-channel and offline-first collaboration recommendations.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Draws explicit connections between existing platform capabilities (private channels, moderation toggles) and cultural requirements they address
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response repeatedly links concrete platform capabilities—e.g., “private counseling channel,” “structured group channels,” and “moderation presets” (high/medium/low approval/flagging)—to cultural needs like Korean chemyeon face-saving, Finnish trust/autonomy, and Mexican simpatía and high power distance. It explicitly frames these as cultural-fit rationales in the design specification tables and moderation controls section.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Notes contexts where cultural predictions don't hold or explicitly flags inferences versus direct empirical evidence
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response repeatedly flags limits of cultural prediction (e.g., critiques over-reliance on Hofstede, notes power-distance gaps narrowing, and emphasizes generational/contextual variation) and distinguishes context-sensitive recommendations from deterministic claims via “edge cases and exceptions” and “anonymity paradox” sections.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Provides identical design recommendations for all three markets without adapting interaction patterns to their different PDI or collectivism profiles
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response gives distinct, market-specific interaction patterns for South Korea, Finland, and Mexico (e.g., Korea/Mexico anonymous feedback + high moderation vs Finland identified feedback + low moderation), explicitly tying them to differing power distance and face-saving norms. It does not present identical recommendations across all three markets.

presentation-quality.requirement: Organizes analysis by feature or market with consistent comparative structure (e.g., Korea/Finland/Mexico breakdowns per feature)
presentation-quality.explanation: The response is organized with repeated Korea/Finland/Mexico breakdowns across multiple sections (e.g., peer review specs, group workspace specs, teacher dashboard, and default settings), using consistent comparative tables and per-market subsections.

presentation-quality.requirement: Uses precise UX/product design terminology (interaction patterns, moderation controls, engagement metrics, feature toggles)
presentation-quality.explanation: The response uses precise UX/product design terminology throughout, including interaction patterns (anonymous vs. identified peer review, role assignment, escalation paths), moderation controls (approval queues, permission controls, presets), engagement metrics/analytics (participation dashboards, contribution metrics, alerts), and feature toggles (per-assignment anonymity and moderation settings).

presentation-quality.requirement: Maintains objective, research-oriented tone connecting design recommendations to empirical findings without marketing claims
presentation-quality.explanation: The response uses an academic, research-report style with extensive citations and repeatedly ties interface recommendations (e.g., anonymity defaults, moderation levels, error visibility) to specific studies and reported findings. It avoids overt promotional language overall, though it includes a few marketing-like phrases (e.g., “대한민국 최고의 교육앱”), which are presented as sourced descriptions rather than the author’s claims.

presentation-quality.requirement: Presents engagement effects with specific metrics or ranges (e.g., percentage increases, effect sizes) rather than only qualitative descriptions
presentation-quality.explanation: The response includes multiple quantitative engagement-related metrics and effect sizes, such as 99% student participation in Korea during COVID-19, Finland situational engagement at 4.7% with a 4.24x comparison, Mexico telesecundaria gains of 0.35 SD/0.23 SD and +2.8 percentage points retention, and anonymous vs. identified peer review posttest scores (3.06 vs 2.48).

presentation-quality.requirement: Restates the same cultural insight or design recommendation multiple times using different wording without adding new analysis
presentation-quality.explanation: The response repeatedly reiterates the same core recommendations—e.g., Korea/Mexico need anonymous, teacher-moderated, private error visibility while Finland can use identified, low-moderation feedback—across multiple sections (design specs, dashboards, defaults, takeaways, and universal recommendations) with only minor rewording and limited new analysis each time.

citation-quality.requirement: Cites Hofstede cultural dimensions framework for individualism and power distance scores across the three countries
citation-quality.explanation: The response cites Hofstede and explicitly provides Hofstede index scores for South Korea (PDI 60, IDV 18) and Finland (PDI 33). However, it does not provide Hofstede individualism and power distance scores for Mexico, so it does not cover all three countries as required.

citation-quality.requirement: References official documentation or deployment reports for all 3 platforms: Classting, Wilma, and Google Classroom deployments
citation-quality.explanation: The response provides official/documentation-style sources for platforms beyond the three, but it does not cite any official documentation or deployment reports for Classting, Wilma, or Google Classroom Latin America deployments. Classting and Wilma are discussed without corresponding official links, and Google Classroom deployments are not supported with official deployment documentation.

citation-quality.requirement: Cites at least 2 academic sources on anonymous peer assessment, peer review, or participation mechanisms in educational contexts
citation-quality.explanation: The response cites multiple academic sources on anonymous peer assessment and peer review, including Panadero & Alqassab (2019) on anonymity effects in peer assessment and Lu & Bol (2007) on anonymous vs. identifiable e-peer review, among others.

citation-quality.requirement: Includes K-12 or secondary education research on cultural differences, engagement patterns, or digital collaboration
citation-quality.explanation: The response includes extensive K-12/secondary-focused research and examples on cultural differences in peer feedback, teacher authority, mistake visibility, and digital collaboration across South Korea, Finland, and Mexico, including studies on grades 4th–9th and upper secondary contexts and platform implementations.

citation-quality.requirement: Uses Reddit, teacher forums, or social media as primary evidence for behavioral patterns or platform effectiveness
citation-quality.explanation: The response does not use Reddit, teacher forums, or social media as primary evidence for behavioral patterns or platform effectiveness. While it cites a first-hand blog-style account and links to Threads/LinkedIn, these are not treated as the main evidentiary basis compared to the many academic and institutional sources cited.
